svg.title: MCP Architecture: Connection Patterns & Auth — Industry Best Practices
svg.text: ① Agent → MCP How an AI agent uses tools
Step 1 Agent starts → connects to MCP Server (MCP initialize handshake)
Step 2 Agent requests tool list (tools/list) → MCP returns only scoped tools
Step 3 LLM sees tool list → decides which tool to call
Step 4 Agent calls tool (tools/call) → MCP re-validates → backend executes
Step 5 Result returned to Agent → Agent continues conversation
Key: Agent never sees tools outside its scope. Calling a hidden tool returns an error before backend is touched.
② MCP → MCP Chained / federated MCP servers
Each MCP has its own identity
Use case: microservice decomposition / separation of concerns
MCP-A receives the agent request → routes to MCP-B (read) or MCP-C (write) based on task type
Each MCP authenticates independently to the backend — no credential sharing between servers
Key: When MCP-A calls MCP-B, it must present its own identity token — cannot forward the upstream agent token.
Use case: complex tasks, parallel workloads
Orchestrator receives complex request → splits into parallel sub-tasks → delegates to specialist sub-agents
Sub-agents connect to their own MCP servers, complete work, return results to orchestrator
Orchestrator aggregates and returns the final response
Key: Sub-agent permissions must be a subset of the orchestrator's permissions (no privilege escalation downward).
④ Vendor → MCP Authentication & Authorization flow
Authentication (AuthN) = Who are you? → Prove identity via key / certificate / token
Authorization (AuthZ) = What can you do? → Enforce via scopes / roles / permission tables
• MCP Protocol (Anthropic 2024) — defines initialize / tools/list / tools/call message format
• OAuth 2.0 / OIDC (RFC 6749/7519) — vendor authentication; supported by Azure AD, Okta, Auth0
• Principle of Least Privilege — every agent/MCP holds only the minimum permissions needed
• Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207) — verify every call, never trust by network location alone
